responseSet.title: Splitting-Off and Outsourcing the Social in Dollaria
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Splitting-Off and Outsourcing the Social in Dollaria

Carlos Padrón MA M.Phil. L.P.

Carlos Padrón

Brooklyn, New York, USA

Carlos Padrón  

I believe that most American psychoanalytic institutes tend to encourage private practice as part of their pedagogical and organizational structure. It is an implicit or explicit expectation of candidates as their training is modeled by the way most experienced analysts work. This emphasis on the private parallels what elsewhere I have called a privatized model of subjectivity and of psychic suffering having its source mostly on intra-psychic dynamics. This reality dissociates clinical practice from the constitutive social dimension of its process, from the ways in which social injustices are at the core of psychic pain, and from the immersion of the psychoanalyst into community work. The social undercurrent of psychoanalysis gets split-off from its institutions. In its theoretical understanding, it becomes a matter of only academic discussion, while its practical sphere is outsourced to other professions such as social work,1 or to candidates who work at the institute’s low fee clinical center for meager wages that end up propelling them directly to private practice after graduation.

Klein said that a splitting of the object cannot happen without a splitting of the ego. In ostracizing the social from its institutional walls, ones that cannot be separated from the guardians of whiteness, scientism, heteronormativity, and social class, a part of the original psychoanalytic soul became inaccessible; a soul represented, for example, by Freud’s free clinics and the intellectual but also clinical experiments in social transformation of analysts such as Fenichel or Reich who tried to integrate psychoanalysis and Marxism. A great part of American institutional psychoanalysis lost touch with an essential part of its original vitality, much in the same way that a self loses its aliveness when it splits and dissociates.

This resulted in stunting the American psychoanalytic voice within the public sphere and the politico- historical challenges and injustices of the world. The appearance of a book such as Elizabeth Danto’s Freud’s Free Clinics becomes so essential but also so surprising within American psychoanalysis because the splitting-off of the social produced in the former a dissociation from its progressive history, its origins in Red Vienna and how it evolved into a sustained engagement of psychoanalysis with questions of social justice in places like South America and what the IPA used to symptomatically call “the rest of the world.

responseSet.title: We Will Never Be the Same Again: Dissociation and Mourning in the Face of Trauma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

We Will Never Be the Same Again: Dissociation and Mourning in the Face of Trauma

Boaz Shalgi PHD

Boaz Shalgi

Hasharon, Israel

Boaz Shalgi  

At the beginning of October, my wife and I were trekking in Europe. Not as fit (or young …) as we used to be, the trek took its toll. But the effort, even the aches and pains, felt pertinent, connecting body and mind, past and present and perhaps even some future awaiting ahead,

Saturday morning. October 7th. We woke up to a first stream - soon to become a deluge - of horrific news from home. An unprecedented number and pace of sirens, rockets and missiles, and then the rumors, soon to become facts, of Hamas terrorists - thousands of them - crossing the border, rampaging communities near the border and a music festival - murdering hundreds, slaughtering babies, raping, savagely mutilating women, men and children.

It was impossible to grasp. There were no words. Not a thought to cling to. We were, no doubt, in a state of shock, though even that obvious fact was far from awareness. In retrospect, it seems our psyche’s major effort was to magically erase what we were hearing, to make it go away. We had become hostages in the kingdom of dissociation.

Two weeks later I was sitting in a hotel in Eilat, talking with a 7-year-old child from Sderot1 who said she was afraid of falling sleep. She told me she already practiced all the breathing techniques she had been taught, as part of growing up in a city that regularly is under missile attack, but to no avail. There was something so endearing about her, and her mother, who was sitting a bit embarrassed beside her. The words burst out of me: have you cried?

She looked surprised. Looked at her mother, who could no longer hold back her own tears. Minutes later, a whole family and a therapist were sitting, crying and looking for words to express the torrent of emotions pouring out of them.

Splitting, Klein believed, has to occur when two contradicting emotions cannot reside in the psyche together without destroying each other and the mind itself. Thus, splitting is the precursor to dissociation. Both these mechanisms are crucial in order to save the mind from what is just too much, from losing itself in face of the unbearable. The equation here is straightforward: if I feel That, I can’t feel anything, anymore.

responseSet.title: The Absence Whose Name is Covid: Trauma, Neglect, and the Primal Split
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Absence Whose Name is Covid: Trauma, Neglect, and the Primal Split

Rachel Sopher LCSW

Rachel Sopher

New York, New York, USA

Rachel Sopher  

The “other” has to be created before it can be perceived (Milner, 1969, p. 404)

For most, it seems, Covid came and Covid went. But for me, Covid is still very much alive, present in its absence. We’ve had no choice but to move on, to live.

And yet.

There is a hole ripped in the knitted together fabric of our shared human narrative, a blank spot we strive valiantly to cover over with meaningful activity and connection. This blank spot is the darkest recess of the primal split.

On Neglect, Adult Onset Trauma, and the Center that Cannot Hold: The Contours of the Primal Split

In adult onset trauma, the process of meaning-making, underwritten by the good object comes under attack, leaving a meaningless void that cannot be filled (Laub, 2005). Covid was a trauma of loneliness, of absence, the absence of the good mother, a trauma of no-one-ness.

But, for those who suffered traumatic, early neglect, Covid recapitulated an old wound, the nameless dread of nonexistence. Before the split into good and bad, prior to even the split into deadness and aliveness, there is the most primal split into existence and nonexistence based on the reliable presence or absence of the primary caretaker (Winnicott, 1945). Speaking of early neglect, Green (1997) wrote that for some patients, “the non-existence, will become, at some point, the only thing that is real” (p. 1082). In this case, the absence of the object is more psychically real than its presence, and this void becomes a protected space of non-being inside, inhibiting the elaboration of internal objects, undoing authentic relating to self and other (Sopher, 2018).

And now I am back in my analyst’s office for the first time.

She wears a mask. I do not.

I lost hold of you inside, I tell her.

responseSet.title: Splitting, Confusion, and Hope
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Splitting, Confusion, and Hope

Sally Swartz PHD

Sally Swartz

Cape Town, South Africa

Sally Swartz  

What happens to us psychologically when an event in the social or political world appears to divide us starkly into opposing camps? We’ve all experienced them: those explosive family dinners, acrimonious encounters with companions and friends that bloom suddenly, sweep us up like a tornado and dump us in a wasteland. How do the bonds that tie us together unravel so dramatically?

Sara and I have worked in various settings together for years - long enough for us to have lived through a number of world-order shifts and rumbles, including a pandemic, global conflicts, and our continuing landscape of trauma and violence. It seemed that we had built a shared set of assumptions of the broad political position to which we both belonged. Occasionally our engagement would turn to what is often referred to as another apartheid: Palestine. Sara is a health worker from two generations of activists, both Jewish and Moslem, joined together decades before in struggle against the South African apartheid regime. She traverses the lines of heritage easily, political cohesion being the cement, the forever architecture.

Then the catastrophe: the one we have lived day after day in our media, our hearts and our nightmares. October 7th. The invasion of Gaza.

Soon after, Sara and I got blown apart. It all came down to me asking a question about a petition when she wanted me to be at one with her anguish about the rising death toll in Gaza. My query landed with her as implying that her view and mine might differ in some way, although this was absolutely not my intention. It carried for her the suggestion that her horror might be open to interpretation. All she wanted from me was a visceral and authentic response that would contain her anguish. In that instant I became an enemy. Devastation and helplessness about Israel and the events in Gaza confronted her with the disintegration of the very foundations of the disorderly order with which she had battled her whole life. Splitting became a form of survival. If I was not with her, I was against her. Her disgust with me was palpable. I was the white disgraced.

responseSet.title: Collective Trauma and Splitting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Collective Trauma and Splitting

Kris Yi PHD Psy.D.

Kris Yi

Pasadena, California, USA

Kris Yi  

During the pandemic, my teenage daughter became a fan of the Marvel Cinematic Universe. In the spirit of camaraderie, I watched many Marvel movies with her. While the numerous characters and the plot lines of the movies are all a blur now, I still remember clearly the broadly sweeping theme characterizing them all - the triumph of good over evil. This division of the fantasy universe into good vs. evil would be less troubling if it did not reflect a real life division in our society and in our psychoanalytic organizations. Each side carries conviction that it is aligned with the good and that the other side is evil. It is easy to see the good vs. evil split in movies as simplistic; it is harder to discern that we are mired in this splitting ourselves.

An example is my reaction to the racism debate raging within psychoanalysis. I feel, within me, an impulse to vilify the other side: As a Korean American, I feel incensed by the old guard voices upholding a white-centric worldview. I feel disgust at them - greedy, self-serving people who are too cowardly to face up to the way structural racism benefits them. My contempt is even more pronounced toward Donald Trump and his supporters. In Donald Trump’s profoundly destructive sociopathy, I find justification for vilifying him and his supporters.

I might be smug with my own splitting, if it were not for one major complication - its consequence can be deadly. In graduate school I watched a short film about the human toll caused by the atomic bombs dropped in Japan during WWII. I felt curiously unmoved. I had a similar reaction some years later when the movie Hiroshima, mon amour was screened at a local analytic institute. I felt little empathy toward the destruction the bomb wrought to Hiroshima or its citizens. In both situations, I was surprised by my reaction, because I had a love of Japanese culture and cherished the experience of living in Japan as a college exchange student.

Over the years I thought about my reaction. The movies activated a dissociated state within me of historical trauma from Japan’s colonization of Korea. Korea was one of the earliest victims of Japan’s imperial ambitions that paralleled those of its European counterparts. When I was growing up in the 60’s and 70’s in Korea, our experiences under Japanese colonial rule were very much alive in the collective Korean consciousness. They were prominently featured in Korean textbooks and public discourse. I still have vivid memories of the TV shows and movies depicting the violent brutality - rape, torture, killings, systematic annihilation of Korean language and culture, etc - with which Japanese overlords ruled Korea. Even as these portrayals did not reflect my personal experience, as I was not yet born during the occupation, I was still absorbing the trauma of the colonization, so palpably present in the fabric of Korean life. In this trauma state, Japan existed simply as evil and as a result the singular significance the atomic bombs held for me was that they ended that evil. In this state, I could not afford the nuance of the distinction between the Japanese civilians perishing in the bombing and the Japanese official policy and its henchmen occupying Korea.

The Growth of Desire in Intersubjective Space

Rachel Kabasakalian-McKay PHD

Rachel Kabasakalian-McKay

Bala Cynwyd, Pennsylvania, USA
rachelkmckay@gmail.com

Rachel Kabasakalian-McKay   Founding board member and Co-Director of the Institute for Relational Psychoanalysis of Philadelphia, and is also on the faculty of the Mitchell Relational Studies Center, the National Institute for the Psychotherapies (NIP) and Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy (ICP) in New York. She is co-editor, with David Mark, of Inhabiting Implication in Racial Oppression and in Relational Psychoanalysis (2023, Routledge). She is in practice in Philadelphia and New York.

Contrasting the work of French child psychoanalyst Françoise Dolto with that of American Relational theorist Jessica Benjamin yields significant shared sensibilities, notably regarding what was in Dolto’s context a radical assertion of the small child as subject who needed to be considered as such, and the role of the parent’s relating and communications in nurturing both subjectivity and agentic desire. However, Benjamin’s writing makes clear not only that the parent’s subjectivity is central to the child’s affective experience, but the waysthe child’s subjectivity takes shape in response to the mix of attunement, rupture, acknowledgment and repair. This makes parenting both riskier and more intimate; children can experience recognition that is inherently mutual, but are also more vulnerable to parental failures of both responsiveness and self-regulation. Relational perspectives are also considered regarding conscious and unconscious parental transmissions as they influence the development of a child’s desire.

This text attempts to underscore the significant role of Francoise Dolto, the French child psychoanalyst, working with traumatized children during and after World War II. Being mostly known as a friend and colleague of Jacques Lacan, her own original clinical work was often overshadowed by the more prominent psychoanalysts on the French scene. Francoise Dolto could be considered as working in a relational mode, but more importantly, she truly put the concept of the symbolic to work, speaking to infants from the beginnings of their lives in an adult language, always invoking and including the instance of the law and the Third in her analytic work.

We are deeply grateful for the benevolent, precise inspiring replies to our article. In our reply we present the research context of our article rooted in a phenomenological analysis of French psychoanalytic approaches to an intercorporeal understanding of the unconscious. We discuss different aspects of the comments by Wolff-Bernstein and Kabasakalian-McKay, namely the role of Hegelian and Lacanian aspects in Doltos psychoanalytic approach, Doltos understanding of the origin and development of the self in intersubjective relations, the dialectics between the individual and the collective in Doltos thinking and the necessary distinction between verbal and nonverbal aspects of communication.

Victor Erice’s Film The Spirit of the Beehive: A Coded Warning Against Submission in the Aftermath of the Spanish Civil War

Julie Gerhardt PHD and Dan Slobin PHD

Dan Slobin

San Francisco, California, USA
jgerhardtphd@gmail.com

Berkeley, California, USA

Dan Slobin   Emeritus Professor of Psychology and Linguistics at the University of California, Berkeley. He is a Fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the American Association for the Advancement of Science, and the Linguistics Society of America. He has published numerous books and papers on language and cognition across languages, with particular attention to child language development. His research sites include Spain (the setting of the film under discussion here), Mexico, Chile, Italy, Germany, The Netherlands, Croatia, Turkey, and Israel.

This essay discusses Victor Erice’s film The Spirit of the Beehive in terms of issues relevant to psychoanalysis today: the impact of socio-political trauma on the psyche. Through his cinematic artform, Erice captures the isolation and emotional deadness of village life in the aftermath of the Spanish Civil War which we discuss in terms of the contemporary interest in intergenerational transmission of trauma as well as the expansion of the psychoanalytic paradigm to include socio-historic events impacting unconscious processes. Through a personal narrative of a young girl’s plight, the film captures her mind-numbing surrender to fantasy elaborations - which serves as an allegory for the people of Spain’s mind-numbed surrender to fascist dogma. Throughout the film, a witness is needed, so Erice turns to us, his viewers to take on this role. Beyond being a coded exposé of Franco’s regime, the film serves as a warning with disturbing relevance today.

responseSet.authorMast: Lauren Levine,  Amy Schwartz Cooney,  Jack Foehl and  Stephen Hartman
responseSet.authorCitation: Levine, L., Cooney, A. S., Foehl, J. & Hartman, S.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 34-3

Lauren Levine PHD , Amy Cooney PHD , Jack Foehl PHD and Stephen Hartman PHD

Stephen Hartman

Stephen Hartman  

It was not so long ago, at least in the United States, that practitioners of the various mental health disciplines who were called upon to work side by side under the same roof were each consigned a signature task and turf. When we four editors of this journal were clocking our hours toward licensure in clinics and hospitals, psychiatrists were, almost always, the team leaders in clinical settings and the primary investigators in research. Social workers attended to systems: families, couples, groups, and to the agencies that provided social services. Psychologists were the data scientists proficient in psychotherapy as well as psychometrics and psychological assessment. Therapy was something we were invited to augment with empirical research, perhaps with some background in philosophy and social science.

This was especially true in psychoanalytic settings. If social workers were at home in the trenches with severely ill patients, and psychiatrists at home in medical roles and offices with windows, psychologists were the number crunchers and projective testers whose home was in the data even when that data was as wildly subjective as the House Tree Person personality assessment. (No wonder, psychologists who turned away from psychoanalysis were among the leaders of a movement for empirically validated treatments). Participation in the hierarchy of mental health disciplines, then as sadly often-still now, meant playing roles in disavowed hierarchies that permeate social and institutional life more generally.

As we four editors were meeting to close this issue of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, 34:3, we recalled that the first projective test we were taught to administer consisted of a simple instruction to the patient: draw a house, a tree, and a person. Their psychic structure, personality structure, neuropsychological fitness, and object relational world were evaluated as if the degree to which their capacity to represent a “home,” a “self,” and an “environment” were drawn with solidity and cohesiveness devoid of the intersectional, sociocultural surround. Relational Psychoanalysis was one of the cracks in the floorboards of the home in which psychologist-as-projective assessor dwelt. Homes became big tents under which families shared a variety of relations. By the time the IARPP assembles in Merida, Mexico, this June to discuss matters of home and belonging, relational psychoanalysts will have long since decamped from an edifice that is tidy and true. The diversity of our personal and professional backgrounds and practices is as noteworthy and critical to the future of our discipline (and to our journals) as the fundamentals of a normative habitus were to the House Tree Person test of our clinical “training.

Affiliation vs. Alienation

Sam Gerson PHD

Sam Gerson

Oakland, Northern California, USA

Sam Gerson   Founder and Past-President of the Northern California Society for Psychoanalytic Psychology (NCSPP) and the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California (PINC) where he is a Training and Supervising Analyst. Dr. Gerson is an Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, and an Editor for the Psychoanalytic Quarterly. His publications have focused on contemporary issues in clinical process; relational approaches to conceptualizing and working with hysteric, narcissistic and masochistic phenomena; and on the transgenerational aftermath of massive trauma. Contemporary psychoanalytic thinking has been marked by a “social turn” – a shift in emphasis from internal and endogenous process to those that prioritize the social and political context in which psychological life is shaped. In this discussion of Rozmarin’s work, the concept of “belonging” is considered as a bridge between the individual and the community. The entities to which one belongs and the dynamics that underline the choices attendant to belonging are considered as constituents of both identity and unconscious processes. An individual’s unconscious is viewed as nested within the broader historical and social configurations of life and structured by these forces. Reference is made to the work of Erich Fromm and Erik Erikson as aides in understanding the power of the social to shape both identity formation and social affiliation. The dynamics of conflict between affiliation and alienation are considered as a constituent of contemporary movement toward authoritarian political structures.

This document delves into the intricate interplay among cultural, geographical, and sociopolitical contexts in shaping identity and individual perceptions, drawing inspiration from Eyal Rozmarin’s seminal work. It discusses the ongoing exploration of these themes within the framework of the IARPP 2024 conference titled “The Quest for Belonging and the Co-creation of a Therapeutic Home.” Focusing on Mexico’s diverse cultural landscape, it examines how varied regional experiences contribute to a complex sense of belonging within the nation. Through narratives of psychoanalysts representing different Mexican regions, it highlights the nuanced ways cultural and social realities mold personal identity and notions of belonging. The discussion underscores the role of psychoanalysts in understanding and shaping relational dynamics, informed by their introspective journeys and unique perspectives. This exploration fosters deeper reflection on the fluid nature of the self and its implications within the Mexican context, offering valuable insights into identity formation and the quest for belonging amid Mexico’s dynamic cultural tapestry.

Dear Eyal

Gohar Homayounpour Psy.D.

Gohar Homayounpour

Tehran, Iran
g.homayounpour@gmail.com

Gohar Homayounpour   Psychoanalyst and award winning author. She is a member of the International Psychoanalytic Association, and the American Psychoanalytic Association. She is a Training and Supervising psychoanalyst of the Freudian Group of Tehran, of which she is also founder and past president. She is a member of the scientific board at the Freud museum in Vienna, and of the IPA group Geographies of Psychoanalysis. Her first book, Doing Psychoanalysis in Tehran (2012, MIT) won the Gradiva award and has been translated into many languages. Her latest book is titled Persian Blues, Psychoanalysis and Mourning (2022, Routledge).

In this paper, Gohar Homayounpour responds in the format of a letter to Eyal Rozmarin’s paper: Belonging and its Discontents, in which he lyrically and psychoanalytically addresses the current war in Gaza, in a deeply personal spirit as an Israeli-American psychoanalyst living under its tragic and disturbing shadow. Homayounpour attempts to question our need for belonging, towards an ethics of un-belonging. The various problematics/seductions of belonging are confronted metapsychologically, but she moves beyond that into a personal/political narrative on the horrific and ongoing tragedy in Israel and Palestine. Where dreams have failed into nightmares, where Radical hope or the ethics of the social as she puts it, becomes more radically indispensable than ever.

responseSet.title: Embracing Complexity and the Need for Inclusive Belongings
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Embracing Complexity and the Need for Inclusive Belongings

Lynne Segal MA PHD

Lynne Segal

Camperdown, NSW, Australia
l.segal@bbk.ac.uk

Lynne Segal   Feminist writer and activist, whose books include Is the Future Female? Troubled Thoughts on Contemporary Feminism; Slow Motion: Changing Masculinities, Changing Men; Straight Sex: The Politics of Pleasure; Why Feminism? Gender; Psychology & Politics; Making Trouble: Life & Politics; Out of Time: The Pleasures & Perils of Ageing; Radical Happiness: Moments of Collective Joy; The Care Manifesto: The Politics of Interdependence(co-authored); Lean on Me: A Politics of Radical Care. As a Jewish activist she has worked for decades on peace and justice for Palestinians, and in general continues to work on the fortunes of feminism, gender, aging, care, solidarity and friendship.

It is an acutely tragic time for many Jewish people who have for decades shared Eyal Rozmarin’s commitment to working for peace in Israel/Palestine. Rozmarin importantly emphasizes the many ties that bind people, often unconsciously, to collectivities, especially the ways in which nation states, or birthplaces, provide identity narratives that unite, or sometimes, discomfort, us. However, it is not so uncommon for people to have fractured belongings, as life experiences complicate our ties to past identifications and attachments. In this essay I discuss the historic complexity of Jewish ties to Israel, including my own. I note that Jewish criticisms of Israel’s dispossession of Palestinians, which have always existed from well before the creation of the state of Israel, are continually dismissed by allowing only one narrative to be addressed, that of political Zionism. All other attempts to criticize Israel’s refusal to contemplate justice for native Palestinians is spuriously undermined as antisemitism. Yet today, more Jewish people are joining with others who are not only currently devastated by Israel’s genocidal aggression in Gaza, but also demanding equal rights and freedom for everyone now residing in Israel/Palestine.

responseSet.title: Belonging on the Edge
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Belonging on the Edge

Eyal Rozmarin PHD

Eyal Rozmarin

eyal.rozmarin@gmail.com

Eyal Rozmarin   Psychoanalyst and writer. He was born in Israel-Palestine and now lives and works in New York. He writes at the intersection of the psychological and the social, about subjects, collectives, and the forces that drive their relations. He is Co-Editor of the book series Relational Perspectives in Psychoanalysis, and on the editorial board of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Psychoanalysis has the capacity to explain how and why people join together and against each other. If we understand identity and identification, if we understand belonging, we might be able to see how we could free ourselves from the visceral forces that pull us into mad, warring factions, making us destroy each other when we could be neighbors.

responseSet.title: The Ice Is Melting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ice Is Melting

Leigh Lyndon Psy.D.

Leigh Lyndon

5665 College Avenue, Suite 220 C, Oakland, CA 94618
leigh@drleighlyndon.com

Leigh Lyndon   Psychoanalyst who maintains a private practice in Oakland. She has taught and supervised at Access Institute, CPMC, and TPI. She also co-facilitates a case conference for Peer Counselors at RAMS as part of her ongoing work with the Community Psychoanalytic Track at PINC. Leigh is a graduate of PINC. In an earlier chapter of her life, she was a choreographer and performing artist in New York City.

In The Ice is Melting, Leigh Lyndon, Psy.D., uses three personal vignettes to illustrate various uses of an object, the process of internalizing an other, and how addiction can be used to circumvent the need for an other. Using auto theoretical prose poetry to pull the reader into her experiences, she allows the reader, thusly, to encounter - rather than be told – her ideas about the ways in which bodies are inscribed not only by our earliest relationships but by the social as well. She describes in detail several ways that she attempted to manage her anxiety about having to be in these different relationships (to self, to the other, to culture). By using auto-theory, Lyndon is implicitly asking the reader to attend to their own sources of understanding and is troubling our ideas of what it is “to know.”

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ice is Melting … in a Drink, Best Served with Ma (間): What Happens When Autotheorist and Reader Meet in the Evocative Space

Maxine Chung BA (HONS)

Maxine Chung

28 North Parade, London, Lincolnshire N5 1RN
maxinemeifungchung@gmail.com

Maxine Chung   Psychoanalytic psychotherapist, author, lecturer and clinical supervisor based in London, UK. Her debut novel, The Eighth Girl, was published in 2021 with William Morrow. What Women Want: Conversations on Desire, Power, Love and Growth was published in 2023 by Hutchinson Heinemann, Penguin Random House. Her second work of non-fiction is due for publication with PRH in 2025. She is a visiting lecturer at The Faber Academy and The John Bowlby Centre

The discussion is a response to Leigh Lyndon’s paper: The Ice is Melting, that locates itself in the genre of autotheory and takes itself as “the case.” The discussion opens up exploration into what happens in the intersubjective third, and Ma (間), when there is joint and felt participation through storytelling. In our joint disclosure, Leigh and I are extending a conversation and engaging in “the case” which is different from disclosure in the employment of enactment. The discussion refers to, but departs from, the traditional case demonstration and invites experimentation with alternative modes of psychoanalytic writing.

Still Life

(To Leigh Garrett Lyndon, Psy.D.)

Masha Armyn PHD

Masha Armyn

127 West 96th St. Suite 1B, New York, NY 10025
masha.borovikova@gmail.com

Masha Armyn   Psychologist in private practice on Manhattan Island, New York, and a sometime candidate in the NYU postdoctoral program in psychoanalysis. She is SAFD-certified in hand-to-hand, broadsword, and rapier & dagger combat, and spends much of her time mothering three dragons and procrastinating writing.

To Leigh Garrett Lyndon, when The Ice Is Melting. Thoughts in return for thoughts, in paper words and perishable ink. This is about love and cycling, love of recycling, nighttime adventuring, and morning regrets. This is about the violence of love, the impermanence of memory, the persistence of remembering, the blackouts, the spaces in between and instead of […]. This is about the unrelenting promise of floating on water.

[[RunningHead]]

Après Coup of “You Must Remember This”

Adrienne Harris PHD , Lauren Levine PHD , Amy Cooney PHD , Jack Foehl PHD and Stephen Hartman PHD

Stephen Hartman

Stephen Hartman  

With the republication of Adrienne Harris’ classic essay, “You must remember this” (Harris, 2009), Psychoanalytic Dialogues continues our new feature, Après Coup. By putting a paper from the early years of the journal in conversation with contemporary readers and writers, we hope to dive into the ways in which psychoanalysts forge relationality in spaces of complexity and contradiction over time, as our experiences- collective, dyadic, or solitary – are always nonlinear and multiply configured. This Apres Coup includes commentaries by Cleonie White, Ph.D. Ken Corbett, Ph.D. Shubha Herlekar, Psy.D. MFT, Victor Donas, M.D. Donnel Stern, Ph.D. and Giuseppe Civitarese, M.D. Ph.D.

[[RunningHead]]

Memory, “Rememory,” Duppies, and Time

Cleonie White PHD

Cleonie White

67-71 Yellowstone Blvd. #7H, Forest Hills, NY 11375, USA
historysmuse@aol.com

Cleonie White   Faculty and supervisor at the William Alanson White Institute of Psychiatry, Psychoanalysis, and Psychology. She is Adjunct Clinical Assistant Professor at New York University’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She sits on the Editorial Board member of Contemporary Psychoanalysis and is an Associate Board member of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. A co-founder of the Study Group on Race and Psychoanalysis at the White Institute, Dr White is also a member of Black Psychoanalysts Speak and was a participant in the film, “Black Psychoanalysts Speak”. Her writing and publications are in the areas of trauma and dissociation, race, class, the immigrant/foreigner Other, identity, and creativity in psychoanalysis. Dr White maintains a private practice in NYC.

How do we negotiate time in the present and hold hope for the future, when the literature of your history, of ancestral traumas housed, unmetabolized, in our bodies, has not as yet been narrated into consciousness? What mediating factors might enable us to negotiate historical and recent loss and, furthermore, facilitate mourning? In this essay, I consider the initial and continued impact on me of first listening to, then re-reading, Adrienne Harris’ evocative presentation, and later publication of, “You Must Remember This” (2009). These revived experiences shepherded me into reflecting spaces, particularly regarding the significance of ghosts, or duppies, as they are referenced in Jamaican culture. Are duppies, in the cultural collective, empowered as links and potential mediators between past and present – as keepers of ancestral secrets that, if newly learned, or “rememoried,” might facilitate subjective and relational transformation? In this regard, the voices of Toni Morrison and Stewart Hall offer contemplative possibilities.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Is it That Insists? Polaroids of a Night in Tunisia with Adrienne Harris

Victor Doñas MD

Victor Doñas

Hernando de Aguirre 194 of 54, Providencia, Santiago, Chile
vito_donas@hotmail.com

Victor Doñas   Medical Doctor and psychiatrist, and a trained psychoanalyst whose practice is based in Santiago, Chile. He is co-founder of the IARPP Collective, founding member of the Ibero-Latin American Relational Group of Psychotherapy and Relational Psychoanalysis (GRILPP), member of the Association of Psychoanalysis and Relational Psychotherapy of Chile (APPR Chile) and the Santiago Psychoanalytical Association (APSAN), as well as former member of the Latin American Institute of Mental Health and Human Rights (ILAS). Doñas has authored several articles in the interfield of Psychoanalysis and Social Theory, has served as editor of Routledge Book Series, and attached editor of the Psychoanalytic Dialogues journal. Dr Doñas is also a dilettante musician and father of 15-year-old Pedro.

Adrienne Harris´ “You Must Remember This” is a propeller to re-encounter the notion of “aprés coup,” a concept that continues to defy analysand’s and analyst’s archetypical constructions. Taking Harris’ invitation to stroll between Tangiers and Casablanca, this piece offers a voyage from the landscape of personal stories to the possibilities within relational clinic, using the savvy of music, film, literature, and philosophy, coalesced in a couple of psychic polaroids. The first polaroid frames the enigmatic presence and movement of memory, the issue of what is it that insists in session. The second, snapshots the bending movements of time and space in liminal experiences at inter-subjective spaces (session-treatment), which demands the analyst to go beyond its own “I” to maintain liminal spaces open for radical alterity to manifest. Finally, the author focuses on the notion of emptiness to create new paths, which may demand the analyst to disown its omnipotence.

Intersubjectivity, Field Theory, and Adrienne Harris’s “You Must Remember This”

Donnel Stern PHD

Donnel Stern

24 East 82nd St., Suite 1B, New York, NY 10028-0871

Donnel Stern   Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute in New York City; and Clinical Professor of Psychology and Clinical Consultant at the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy. He is Founder and Editor of a book series at Routledge, “Psychoanalysis in a New Key,” which has over 85 books in print. He is the former Editor-in-Chief of the journal Contemporary Psychoanalysis. His most recent book is The Infinity of the Unsaid: Unformulated Experience, Language, and the Nonverbal (Routledge, 2019). A new book will appear later this year: On Coming Into Possession of Oneself: Transformations of the Interpersonal Field.

Adrienne Harris’s “You must remember this” has attained classic status, and for good reason: It is one of the most elegant and brilliant statements of intersubjectivity in the psychoanalytic literature. After reviewing the accomplishment of the article, I offer some ancillary thoughts on field theory.

The author emphasizes Adrienne Harris’s skillful use of autobiographical material in his writing, an aspect that contributes to making it a remarkable essay. He also argues that any psychoanalytic writing about is invariably autobiographical, and that this is also the way, in Walter Benjamin’s phrase, to move from mere chronicle to history. Nothing in a psychoanalytic essay tastes true unless it has first been immersed in the subjectivity of the narrator.

[[RunningHead]]

Racial Rage, Racial Guilt: The Uses of Anger in Asian America

David L. Eng PHD and Shinhee Han PHD

Shinhee Han

Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, USA
Fisher-Bennett Hall 3340 Walnut Street, Philadelphia, PA 19104
deng@english.upenn.edu

New York, New York, USA

Shinhee Han   Senior psychotherapist in Counseling Services at The New School and in private practice in New York City. She is the co-author (with David L. Eng) of Racial Melancholia, Racial Dissociation: On the Social and Psychic Lives of Asian Americans (Duke, 2019). Han is also a founding member of the Asian Women Giving Circle in New York City, a philanthropic organization that funds Asian women artists creating social activism and change. Previously, she was a therapist at the University of Chicago, Northwestern University, Barnard College, and Columbia University.

Asian Americans are conventionally described as “middle-man minorities,” outside of dominant racial paradigms of White and Black, adjunct to White privilege and exempt from the brunt of systemic violence directed against Black people. Historical accounts of the in-betweenness of Asian Americans trace their origins to how Asian coolie labor has served to triangulate White capital and African slavery over the course of European modernity. If this is the material history of in-betweenness, what is the psychic corollary of the middle-man thesis? Through an analysis of the Netflix dark comedy series Beef, as well as case histories of Asian American patients and students, we argue that the psychic effects of occupying a racially intermediate position implicate an unexplored terrain of racial rage and racial guilt that Asian Americans are insistently socialized to hold on behalf of others.

[[RunningHead]]

The Stork

Stephen Hartman PHD

Stephen Hartman

San Francisco, California, USA
stephen@eshartman.com

Stephen Hartman   Joint editor-in-chief of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and former co-editor of Studies in Gender and Sexuality. He teaches at the NYU Postdoctoral Program where he co-chairs the Relational Track and at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. In addition to his recent book, Reading with Muriel Dimen/Writing with Muriel Dimen: Experiments in Theorizing a Field, he has written extensively about technology and psycho-social experience as it pertains to gender, object relations, the psychoanalytic frame, and citizenship. Stephen practices in New York and San Francisco; his yoga mat and road bike are parked in Brooklyn.

Taking my trip to a maternity ward to visit my newborn niece as a “clinical case,” I visit the phenomenon of digitally surveilling neonates with the self-same devices that were designed to enforce the house-arrest of would-be felons whose noisy place in the cultural imaginary has rendered the neonate’s ankle a transgenerational “risk object” in the unconscious collective. In conversation with the ever-emergent genre known as autotheory, I invite the reader to be an accomplice as I “dramatize my unsettlement” contrasting autotheory with Laplanchean “self-theorization” via interludes about temporality, expertise, birth, gender, race, and place that come to life in the tale of The Stork.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 34-5

Jack Foehl PHD , Amy Schwartz Cooney PHD , Stephen Hartman PHD and Lauren Levine PHD

Lauren Levine

Lauren Levine  

It’s weird. If you stop to think about it, world over, politicians, pundits, and pollsters tend to address the public in binaries. “The party is wrong/right.” “I have a better plan.” “She/he/they speak(s)for the people.” Follow this thread, and you’ll notice how rare it is to hear a shift from a dyadic structure to a triadic one.

Enter the United States Vice Presidential candidate, Tim Walz joining Kamala Harris on the Democratic ticket at just the moment when a total breakdown felt imminent. By contrast to the harrowing threat, issued from both sides of the aisle and in media of all stripes, that nothing less than democracy is at state, there was this:

You know there’s something wrong with people when they talk about freedom: freedom to be in your bedroom, freedom to be in your exam room, freedom to tell your kids what they can read. That stuff is weird.

By contrast to the flagrantly racist, identity-based attacks of the Trump/Vance ticket, Walz’s comments playfully invite the potential of a third, a way out of the doer/done-to (Benjamin, 2018) dynamics of politics-as-usual. “That’s weird” gestures toward a triadic space for reflection on stark values and policy differences – “calling in,” and recognizing the basic humanity of the public who might just be able to think about “weird” stuff after all.

The auspicious launch of the Harris/Walz campaign has injected a spark of joy into a campaign formerly filled with dread. True: Walz lapses into split complementarities when, for example, he claims Trump is “weird as hell.” But as much as we try to resist complementarities and splits, to veer away from reductive binaries, we all lapse into binaries as Benjamin writes, because recognition is only ever unevenly realized. Still: only a former high-school teacher could work this stroke of Winnicottian genius. Were Trump to reply, “I’m not weird, you are!,” even the most inveterate partisan would have no choice but to laugh. And this, just when potential space seemed all but belly-up in US politics, as tragically in much of the world. Might “weird” be an antidote to breakdown?

responseSet.title: It Takes Two to Know One: Mutual Relations at the Heart of Therapeutic Action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

It Takes Two to Know One: Mutual Relations at the Heart of Therapeutic Action

Anthony Bass PHD

Anthony Bass

USA
315 W 57 St., Suite 305, New York, NY 10019
tony.bass@gmail.com

Anthony Bass   Adjunct Associate Professor and a clinical consultant at the New York University Postdoctoral Program for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy. He is also on the faculty and a training and supervising analyst at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. He was a founding director of IARPP and the Stephen Mitchell Center for Relational Studies, where is on the faculty and president of the board of directors. He is on the board of directors of the Sandor Ferenczi Center of New York at the New School for Social Research. He is an editor emeritus of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

In this essay, I reflect on the origins of relational psychoanalysis during my training years at the NYU Postdoctoral Program between 1983 and 1989, and the subsequent evolution of my own psychoanalytic credo in which I place the essential mutuality of the psychoanalytic process at the heart of the therapeutic action that it generates. I explore the idea that all analysis is at heart a form of mutual analysis in which personal transformation and healing is inevitably bi-directional.

responseSet.title: The Experience of Breakdown
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Experience of Breakdown

Anne Alvarez PHD

Anne Alvarez

Cornwall, UK

Anne Alvarez  

Winnicott (1974) tells us that fear of breakdown is a feature of significance in some of our patients, but not others. The term is vague, he says, but he means a “failure of a defence organization where there is a breakdown of the establishment of the unit self.” He adds a brief lesson on the early stages of emotional growth and points out that “the facilitating environment has a kind of growth of its own, being adapted to the changing needs of the growing individual” (Winnicott, 1974, p. 104). I think this latter point has many implications for our technique.

Winnicott contends that clinical fear of breakdown is “the fear of a breakdown that has already been experienced”. (p. 104) He asks, “Why does the patient go on being so worried by this that belongs to the past? (p. 105) “The answer must be that the original experience of primitive agony cannot get into the past tense unless the ego can first gather it into its own present time experience. And into omnipotent control now. (assuming the auxiliary ego-supporting function of the mother (analyst)” in the transference.) [I prefer the word `potent` to `omnipotent` because it connotes a more progressive, rather than defensive or problematic process.]

Winnicott lists a variety of primitive agonies, and then adds, Fear of death and then Emptiness. “This emptiness needs to be experienced and this emptiness belongs to the past, to the time before the degree of maturity had made it possible for emptiness to be experienced.”

To understand this, it is necessary to think not of trauma but of nothing happening when something might have happened. It is easier for a patient to remember trauma than to remember nothing happening when it might have happened. (see Music, 2009, on neglect)

I have frequently written about a turning point in my autistic patient Robbie’s treatment after a radical change in my technique, the reclamatory and vitalizing effect of which manifested itself in the session, and over the many years to come. I did, however (in Alvarez, 1992 and earlier) recount the fact that the day after our dramatic session, at the age of 13, Robbie had a breakdown- or breakout – from his autism where he sobbed continuously from his bed while telling his parents about the terrible experience he had had at 18 months of age when he was sent away to acquaintances of theirs in the country after his mother had been rushed to hospital with severe pneumonia (see Alvarez, 1992, 2012; Schwartz Cooney & Sopher, 2021).

responseSet.title: Blackout
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Blackout**

Daniel G. Butler L.M.F.T. PHD

Daniel G. Butler

San Francisco, California, USA

Daniel G. Butler  

I pray for the light to take me

God knows the morning won’t wake me

I dream in diazepam

Let me leave without a sound

I swear to God, no kidding

This might be life but this ain’t living

I only think of the sin

I’ll only do it again

And again …

– High Vis, “Out Cold”

The dark may be liked and blackness may be for some the best color, but black also denotes something evil, terrifying, traumatic.

– Winnicott, “Hallucination and Dehallucination”

1997. Seventeen-year-old white kid (A.) emerges from a blackout with a man in blue scrubs requesting his belt and shoelaces. A. was alert from the speed but delirious from downers for weeks. He thought he was just having fun, but now he knows “fun” was a euphemism for blackouts that helped him survive. His friend and drug dealer, B., landed him in the hospital. B., of all people, first sounded the alarm that A. was spiraling. Friends used to joke about B. smoking so much speed in the high school bathroom that it plumed into the hallway. Fast forward thirty years, and the laughter has died. B. died with it (you know how) at 36.1

responseSet.title: The Wish for Breakdown
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Wish for Breakdown

Ken Corbett PHD

Ken Corbett

New York, New York, USA

Ken Corbett  

Patients often come into treatment with the wish that analysis will produce a breakdown – “a stark shock of embodied rapture, the angel dropping from the sky to smack [them] with clarity’s two-by-four” (Akbar, 2024, p. 82) – or add depth to the breakdown that brought them to the consulting room door. On their knees, awakened by the divine or a less heavenly smack, patients arrive pursuing their troubled pasts lived now in the present (nobody thinks of now as the future past) in the hope that they will break through their deadening fixations, their buzzing fugues, their compelling yet unproductive hatreds, their wish to save and save and save those who do not wish to be saved, their quest to repair those who cling to unrepaired and unrepentant ways, neglecting and abusing now as then. Loyalties are hard to foreswear. Still, patients seek to separate from the circular pull of their unmet desires, and to relinquish the dogged defenses that keep those longings alive. They push ahead in an effort to rebuild, wishing for clarity and a way to regroup, hoping to find their way out of depression, dissociation, and rage, while also struggling with the complex healing of grief, reconciliation, recognition and gratitude. If only it were so, or so easily so, if only rebuilding were that tidy and linear, if only inertia were that easy to disrupt – repetition not epiphany is the language of psychoanalysis – round and round we go, defenses flail, integration frays, defenses regroup, hate flares, the past in the present revolts and reignites, sometimes with insight (or at least information) but more often with blunted insistence and torpor. (“Why have a feeling when you can just be sick?” as my analyst was fond of saying.) Still, given enough time, the past slumps off, and sometimes grief, recognition and gratitude follow, yes, but if only recognition and gratitude were the faithful baseline to which one returned after passing through a requisite interval of grief, if only grief were an interval and not a specter, if only grief and gratitude were not entangled in conflicting social realities and political propositions.

responseSet.title: Fear of Breakdown and Object Usage: Confronting and Surviving Destructiveness, with Some Help from the Maternal
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fear of Breakdown and Object Usage: Confronting and Surviving Destructiveness, with Some Help from the Maternal

Jill Gentile PHD

Jill Gentile

New York, New York, USA

Jill Gentile  

We are living in breakdown times. What is new, exciting, and terrifying is the growing evidence that, on a collective scale, we are no longer merely plagued by our fears of breakdown: we are actually courting breakdown. Take, for example, a recent NYT Siena college poll which reveals that 70% of those polled, across both political parties, either want major change to the U.S. economic and political system or want to tear the system down altogether (Barbaro, 2024). Can we, then, speak of “breakdown desire,” and not only “breakdown fear,” as a collective political force? And what of our clinical responsibility (Gentile, 2023) to cultivate disruption (a form of breakdown) as integral to therapeutic action in our practices?

If breakdown fears and desires—across both the clinical and political realms—increasingly preoccupy our patients, ourselves, and psychoanalysis itself, then we have an opportunity as clinicians to stay curious and engage with them. Meandering with Winnicott, might we play with his themes, placing them perhaps in a novel arrangement: breakdown, object use, survival, and possibly revival of the Mother? Let’s try.

As I’ve previously argued, the constellation of recent political turbulence, continued assaults on democracy and the planet, global racialized uprisings, and the pandemic not only exhausted us and splintered the fabric of daily life, producing enormous psychic and material tolls, most especially for already oppressed subjects, it also enlisted us—en masse—into something of a collective psychoanalytic journey (Gentile, 2020). By means of destabilizing temporalities, novel paradoxes, and uncanny phenomena, we alternately, or at the same time, sharpened our psychic conditioning, sovereignty, and discernment and potentiated a vulnerability to omnipotent self-capture and fascistic states of mind. A marked political chasm has deepened, catalyzing wildly disparate political visions, ranging from dire and antidemocratic to radically inclusive and equality seeking. Yet, it seems, we share a desperate longing for release from a stale status quo, insistent on structural change. Perhaps a global process of collective awakening and reckoning has launched, a process that has its own animate, possibly unstoppable momentum. Might we be entering a period of transformative potential beyond the constraints of conventional knowledge and experience?

responseSet.title: Breakdown and Refusal: A Palestinian’s Reflection on Loss and Liberation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Breakdown and Refusal: A Palestinian’s Reflection on Loss and Liberation

Lama Khouri D.P.S.A. L.C.S.W-R.

Lama Khouri

New York, New York, USA

Lama Khouri  

Decolonizing Consciousness and Confronting Genocide in Gaza

Winnicott’s “Fear of Breakdown” as well as his chapter on “Ego Distortions in Terms of True and False Self” (Winnicott, 1965, 1974), verbalize a reality I have lived. Winnicott’s “Fear of Breakdown” explores the idea that individuals fear a psychological breakdown that actually occurred in their past but was never fully processed or acknowledged consciously, leaving unresolved trauma that causes ongoing distress. In his theory of “Ego Distortions in Terms of False and True Self,” he describes how individuals create a “false self” to adapt to environmental failures and meet external expectations, often at the expense of suppressing their genuine “true self.” This leads to a disconnection that impedes authentic expression and causes deep identity and fulfillment issues.

The World Order and the Global Holding Environment

In exploring Winnicott’s concepts within a broader socio-political framework, we see a stark parallel between his psychological theory of a “holding environment” and the global mechanisms of international law and human rights conventions. Ideally, these international structures should function as a global holding environment, providing the necessary safety and stability for communities to develop and thrive.

However, the reality, as seen through the lens of necropolitics, reveals a more sinister dynamic. Achille Mbembe’s analysis of necropolitics allows us to understand that these failures are not merely oversights but are components of a systematic approach employed by states to exert control over life and death (Mbembe, 2003). This deliberate undermining of the global holding environment, particularly evident in the handling of the Gaza genocide, exemplifies how powerful nations manipulate these structures to sustain geopolitical dominance, effectively deciding the survivability of marginalized populations. By linking Winnicott’s theory with necropolitical practices, we uncover how the international community’s negligence mirrors the catastrophic failures in the maternal-infant relationship, perpetuating cycles of trauma and resistance among Palestinians.

responseSet.title: “Dying to Live”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Dying to Live”

Ryan M. Kull PHD

Ryan M. Kull

New York, New York, USA

Ryan M. Kull  

People were bodies, I thought; the only valuable people have beautiful bodies; since my body isn’t beautiful, I’m worthless.

Tom – coming across these words by gay author, White (1988) – recounts to me the ensuing inconsolable state he experienced. “Reading this,” Tom tells me, “was validating all the horrible things that I thought about the world and the gay world and myself. That, you know, I’m not in good physical shape, and I’m worthless.”

In revisiting my recorded sessions with Tom – who participated in my psychoanalytically informed study of adult gay men’s suicidal despair – I found myself associating to Winnicott’s prayer, “May I be alive when I die” (2016, p. 351). Tom, I thought, is already dead when alive (in the biological sense). “I woke up and had a very weird feeling that I was already dead,” he tells me in the following session. “I didn’t feel like I wanted to kill myself. It was like I was living in a world and people could meet me and I could talk to people, but they didn’t realize that I was already dead.” Three weeks later, Tom voluntarily admitted himself to a psychiatric ER, fearing that he was in some kind of danger – manifesting as vague fears that he would kill himself.

According to Winnicott (1974), the mother’s effective holding of the unintegrated infant as it bears primitive agonies allows for the gradual development of non-psychotic defenses, gathering those “unthinkable state of affairs” (p. 103) into the infant’s own omnipotence. Effective maternal holding nurtures numerous adaptations that Winnicott believed are necessary to go on being and feel aliveness. It also allows for actual breakdowns in life that can help one regain a sense of the real – with fears of breakdown signifying a relentless search for experiencing a holding that never happened. Winnicott stated that many people “spend their lives wondering whether to find a solution by suicide, that is, sending the body to death which has already happened to the psyche” (p. 106). Further helped by Ogden’s (2014) translation of fears of breakdown into healthy strivings to live unlived lives, I hear my patients’ suicidal despair as searches for life on distant worlds.

responseSet.title: Fear of Breakdown – the Dissolution of Fear
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fear of Breakdown – the Dissolution of Fear

Rina Lazar PHD

Rina Lazar

Tel Aviv, Israel

Rina Lazar  

7th of October early in the morning in Tel Aviv. We got up to the sounds of sirens. It took some hours to get a glimpse of understanding of what is going on here. However, the first thought that came to my mind while staying in a tiny walk-in closet under the stairs in my house and listening to the radio through my cellular was: It shouldn’t have happened. We are now at the gates of Hell. Nothing will be the same.

Writing nowadays in Israel about anything at all and especially about “fear of breakdown” seems hard. We are there and unfortunately there is no end to our breaking down. Not here and most certainly not in Gaza. There it is “really real:” “Fire has fallen from heaven” and ruined all.

Knowing where I live, the words “Never Again” (relating to the Holocaust) were written on our souls. Knowing that this is Israel’s founding Trauma, it felt as if there were only one way, no other: an all-out war. Though desperately wishing for another way – recognizing our defeat and seeking some way to agreement – I knew that our fear of breakdown was horrendously realized.

However, that was only the beginning.

Living through these eight months, one comes to realize that the Fear of Breakdown is not the worst of all – the Dissolution of Fear surpasses it

What am I talking about? Let me explain through a little vignette and some thoughts.

Daniel an Israeli man, came to meet me seven months after the 7th of October. He lives in the area surrounding the Gaza strip. His place wasn’t invaded by the Hamas’ terrorists, though there were some attempts. After 30 hours in the “safe room” they were evacuated to the center of Israel and after some months to a place in the North and then back to his place of living. All this time he worked with other evacuees. Many of his friends, acquaintances and patients were raped or murdered or abducted or lost family members – hundreds of them. He talked about it in a dense flow of speech and seemingly very contained. And then he said: “I can’t touch this pain. Not yet. My rage blocks the way. I am so furious with our government in almost too concrete a way that the moment I start talking about it I feel that I’m on the verge of breaking down. I hope that it’s ok to talk about it here.” I nodded in agreement and added that I feel the same [this is the level of anonymity, neutrality and abstinence that I follow these days].

responseSet.title: The Breakdown That Already Happened: The Quickening of the Social Collapse in Israel on the Eve of October 7th
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Breakdown That Already Happened: The Quickening of the Social Collapse in Israel on the Eve of October 7th

Esther Rapoport Psy.D.

Esther Rapoport

Tel Aviv, Israel

Esther Rapoport  

For Winnicott, the fear of breakdown can be traced to the breakdown that already took place in infancy, when no mental capacities were present to experience it consciously. The past breakdown, Winnicott tells us, is projected into the future because the ego cannot yet “gather it into its own present time experience” (Winnicott, 1974, p. 105). The person then lives in fear of a breakdown that has already occurred, but which he did not experience (Ogden, 2014).

I would like to take this opportunity to reflect on my patients’ fears of breakdown on the eve of the October 7th massacre and Israel’s subsequent war of annihilation against Gaza, and on my immense subjective difficulty, as a Russian-born therapist who had already lived through a dissolution of a state (Soviet Union), sitting with these fears, through the lens of Winnicott’s ingenious formulation. In the activist communities, I belong to in Israel, the belief was long held that things would need to get worse before they got better: the problematic foundations on which the state of Israel had been built would need to come undone before the positive change we believed in and were working for – a shared society in which Palestinians and Israeli Jews would have equal rights and freedoms – could come about. Unlike the non-activists, we did expect the state to come crumbling down at some point and did not reject that process on a conceptual level. We anticipated the breakdown of the structures, so to speak – but that does not mean that we had the capacity to contain the primary dread that would accompany that process. So, I was not fully shocked when October 7th happened, nor did I – and in that my perspective differed from that of many of my patients – want things to go back to how they looked on October 6th. But that did not ease my horror and pain at the sight of the unbearable atrocities on both sides.

My not being shocked made me a very bad Israeli. The social norm that coalesced very quickly following October 7th required everyone to feel as if the massacre had come out of the blue. You were expected to be completely ahistorical in your emotional reactions to it. Even the mere word “context” became an anathema.

responseSet.title: Inside Out and Upside Down: Winnicott’s Fear of Breakdown
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inside Out and Upside Down: Winnicott’s Fear of Breakdown

Joyce Slochower PHD A.B.P.P.

Joyce Slochower

New York, New York, USA

Joyce Slochower  

Winnicott had a delightfully idiosyncratic way of engaging paradox and playing with ideas. Indeed, it was Winnicott’s wont to turn clinical phenomena on their heads; he regularly surprised us by introducing the unexpected as he reconceptualized psychodynamics. His clinical formulations were at once refreshing, wise, and unexpected.

What at first examination appears pathological or regressive is instead a complex unconscious emotional communication that, when named and understood, has enormous therapeutic potential. This way of inverting psychopathology is evident across much of his thinking.

Winnicott’s “Fear of breakdown” was a particularly important version of this kind of re-invention. Fear of a future breakdown is actually the sequelae of unprocessed historical trauma: what looks like fear of a yet-to-be experienced event is an expression of un-remembered primitive agonies. Encoded – if at all – procedurally, those terrors lack the verbal links required to process, no matter work through, their impact. A frightening future resonates – indeed, reevokes – unbearable past experiences. Because this early breakdown cannot be consciously accessed, no matter named, it is evaded (sometimes by analyst as well as patient); the analytic couple may even retreat to a false self analysis in an unconscious attempt to occlude what lies beneath.

Winnicott noted that other fears – of death, emptiness, and nonexistence – are sometimes similarly experienced. Only when the dyad touches what was disavowed can the patient move beyond these early terrors.

… if the patient is ready for some kind of acceptance of this queer kind of truth, that what is not yet experienced did nevertheless happen in the past, then the way is open for the agony to be experienced in the transference, in reaction to the analyst’s failures and mistakes … (Winnicott, 1974, p. 105)

Ironically, then, access to fear of breakdown implies hope and opens the way to change: As defenses soften, it becomes possible to encompass and then move beyond the early trauma. Pivotal here is the patient’s trust in the analyst to provide what was missing in childhood.

responseSet.title: The Internal Objects of Apartheid
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Internal Objects of Apartheid

Sally Swartz PHD

Sally Swartz

Cape Town, South Africa

Sally Swartz  

There are folds in time, created by trauma. They foreclose the future, the blossoming of choice and ambition. They foreclose the rich tapestry of the past, channeling events into a single path, leading to the catastrophe, the breakdown, an encounter with “O,” with the Real. In this fold, the present constantly repeats. As Stolorow puts it, “Experiences of emotional trauma become freeze-framed into an eternal present in which one remains forever trapped, or to which one is condemned to be perpetually returned by what I call portkeys to trauma” (Stolorow, 2019). Bion describes just such a portkey, in his experience of the death of Corporal Sweeting on the Amiens-Roye Road on August 8, 1918 (Bion, 2018). It was a moment in which he “died.” And surely folds in time are a kind of death. As Adrienne Harris puts it, “So, Bion, in every waking minute, including the careful unfolding minutes of analytic sessions, is both changing, remembering, and still walking on that road, where his friend is still, in every present minute, being killed and he (Bion) not moving, or saving, or helping. And so forever, walking and dying on that road, both men” (Harris, 2012, p. 1). We are all familiar with folds like these – the moment of a death, a crash, a slip, and a fall. Moments such as these repeat as flashbacks and ruminations; we refer to them as “life-changing.” For as long as these moments repeat, they break down our experience of time.

But the breakdown to which Winnicott refers, and about which I write, is fundamentally different to Bion’s moment of dying, or Stolorow’s “portkey” event. My concern is with the internal objects of apartheid. I am describing an internal world split apart by experience of racism from the very beginning of life. This split parallels Bromberg’s description of the origins of dissociative states, or Donnel Stern’s “unformulated experience” (Bromberg, 2003; Stern, 1997). There is no road to Amiens, but an assault beyond or before words.

Winnicott’s fold in time has paradoxically never happened in a strictly biographical sense – it is created before memory is reliably laid down for retrieval, and before established links between psyche and soma. Winnicott describes his fold as a “thing of the past:” “thing” is a telling choice of word here. The “thing” is an object, an internal object, a relationship that happens over and over in its exciting and rejecting affective storms (Fairbairn, 2018). The internal objects of apartheid come into being through race constructed as dangerous or impinging difference. They are the mother hunching her body over her newborn to protect them both from the gaze of an Other, constructed as a threat by virtue of racial categories. They are the father taking the pram across a road to avoid a throng of shadows into which he has imagined an attack. The avoidance, the startle, the quick redirection of footfall inevitably becomes an internal relationship for the child. But how to make sense of such a ubiquitous unspoken danger? Because it is everywhere – the shops and streets, in homes and cars and hospitals. It is a connection at once dependably present and yet foreclosed by avoidance. It is full of mystery and tantalizing, carrying the amplifying of the forbidden.

responseSet.title: Everything is Just Fine?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Everything is Just Fine?

Liat Tsuman PHD

Liat Tsuman

New York, New York, USA

Liat Tsuman  

In the quiet of my office, Dorit’s arrival follows a familiar routine. She enquires about my well-being as she settles on the couch directly across from me. “I’m okay. Everything is just fine,” she declares, her eyes locking onto mine with an intensity that binds and suffocates. Her stare, like a muted scream, speaks of desperation; it feels intolerable to hold and impossible to release. We teeter on the edge of an agony that cannot yet be named.

“Everything is just fine” echoes the hollow contentment of Dorit’s life. She is financially secure, with ample leisure, and in a stable relationship. Yet something is missing; her life is just fine but not much more. Devoid of strong desires or motivations, Dorit wonders, is there more to life? Occasionally, Dorit’s emotional monotony is shattered by waves of all-consuming anxiety. Triggered by interpersonal conflicts, her dreadful fear surfaces, leaving her terrified that small rifts she cannot mend will abruptly end relationships.

The “breakdown [that] has already happened,” but has yet to be experienced (Winnicott, 1974, p. 105) resonates as I remember Dorit’s childhood on a Kibbutz where children lived in a special children’s house. They were raised in small groups and cared for by designated caretakers. Beyond the nursing period, children typically saw their parents once a day for an afternoon visit.

Our sessions follow a familiar rhythm. There is an animating crisis, some foray into the exploration of rich and generative material, but then the conversation ebbs, returning us to the painful place of nothingness, marked by Dorit’s penetrating stare.

My reverie takes me to another kind of stare: to my son, nursing him before bedtime, his gaze unfocused, peaceful. Is this what Winnicott (1962) meant by “unintegration,” I wonder? The state wherein the parent’s “ego-supportive function being taken for granted” allows the child to relax and be free of the need to integrate? (p. 61). Ogden (2014) underscores that for Winnicott the capacity to move between states of disintegration, depersonalization, and letting go of the urge to exist is a healthy condition when experienced in the context of the parent-infant tie. It is in the absence of such a bond that a return to unintegration becomes agony.

responseSet.title: The Fear of Breakdown and Racial Grief
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fear of Breakdown and Racial Grief

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra PHD

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra

Chestnut Hill, Massachusetts, USA

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra  

“No time to grieve. No time to feel your anger. You just keep going. That’s what we do in my world.” Brittany, an African American woman in her 30s, shares these words and then sits silently in our session. She had been talking about watching her cousin get arrested for a crime he did not commit. Her cousin was eventually released from police custody; however, the pain of the injustice lingers for Brittany. She proceeded to tell me that her family always “did things by the book,” in a conscious effort to avoid racism. Her cousin’s arrest marked a moment of reckoning when her privately held fears came to be felt viscerally. Her fears always lie just beneath the surface and this time, she felt hopeless.

Brittany’s agony amidst a racist society speaks to a long-standing collective collapse centered on slavery and racism. As she noted in one session, “You never know when someone will try to pull one over you when you’re Black.” She and her family have little choice but to protect themselves within a racialized society. They anticipate what may happen, based on what has already happened to someone in the family or other African Americans. There is little time and space to mourn.

Winnicott, in the Fear of Breakdown (1974), articulates how a patient’s fear of breakdown signifies a lack of experiencing something that happened in early life amidst a disruption in the facilitating environment. He states that the patient “must go on looking for the past detail which is not yet experienced. This search takes the form of a looking for this detail in the future” (p. 105). Such a nuanced perspective is too easily diminished in contemporary U.S. society which typically assumes a linear, finite process of mourning where a person is supposed to resolve painful experience on their own, forgetting rather than remembering. There is a societal expectation that suggests, “get over it fast.” For Brittany and so many people of color, the repeated message to “get over” histories of slavery and colonization is all too familiar. An additional set of messages suggest that we should not speak out against damaging attitudes and behavior (e.g., violence, bias) within our homes and communities, as any negative impressions of us from the broader world will dismantle us. These messages implicate a fragility, that things can fall apart quickly if we mourn and challenge injustice. In these states of fragility, the fear of breakdown is active within and outside of our homes and communities.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kinship Object: A Creation Engendered from the Bewilderment of a Pillaged World

Lisa Koshkarian PHD and Chris Larralde BFA

Chris Larralde

2211 Post Street, #314, San Francisco, CA 94115
drlisakosh@yahoo.com

Chris Larralde   Graduate of the University of Washington School of Design and Photography and currently serves as a Creative Director for tech companies, where he crafts innovative branding systems and design solutions. Outside of his work, he dedicates time to composing and playing classical and rock guitar music, practicing internal martial arts, and meditation. Additionally, he nurtures his garden and enthusiastically explores both eastern and western philosophies as an avid independent student. Influenced by his Japanese heritage, Chris aspires to a world characterized by increased acceptance, peace, and harmony.

Humankind is reaching a breaking point whereby the prevailing forces of relating are wholly characterized by greed, hate and domination. This worldview seems fixed and objectifies and reduces other humans who are perceived as different, as well as animals, birds, insects, trees and plants as lesser and expendable. But what if the universal attitude were one of kinship with all dynamic beings? A stance which values all forms of living matter as one of us; where nothing has greater value than another form of living matter, making each an equal part of the whole. A stance where relationships are characterized by reverence, reciprocity and the real sense that there is abundance – that there is enough to go around. Out of a sense of grief and horror over the failure of humans to love all of earth’s constituents, we have developed a novel construct presented here: a Kinship Object. This kinship object, an original psychoanalytic idea, is introduced to frame the capacity for balance with regard to relating with maturity and care to all sentient beings. The processes of possessing love and hate, concern, selfhood and otherness, and the thinking and kinship apparatuses are embedded in the kinship object.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Alterity, Kinship, and Repair: When Psychoanalysis Attends to the Natural World

Shifa Haq PHD

Shifa Haq

2315, Hudson Lane, New Delhi 110009, India
shifahaq@gmail.com

Shifa Haq   Psychoanalytic psychotherapist and assistant professor of psychology in the School of Human Studies at Ambedkar University Delhi. Her book, In Search of Return – Mourning the Disappearance in Kashmir was published in 2021. She is an associate editor in the journals, Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Psychoanalysis, Culture & Society and serves in the IPA Subcommittee Migration and Refugees. She writes about psychoanalysis, violence and mourning.

This commentary engages with Lisa Koshkarian and Chris Larrald’s proposal for a “kinship object” to theorize the human- nonhuman connectedness in psychoanalysis. The authors suggest that early experiences with the maternal environment augments the need for companionship, reciprocity, and the capacity for concern paving way for an inclination toward responsibility, reparation, and recognition of the alterity of the other. It is suggested that tolerating subjective otherness not only connects us with members of one’s own species but a consolidating kinship with all others. It is argued that the authors ecological standpoint heralds an ecopoetics of extinction-survival toward possibilities of non-hierarchical entanglements of the human and the non-human elements in the natural world.

The Good Object Process as Developmental Achievement

Rachel Sopher LCSW

Rachel Sopher
