Rachel Sopher   Faculty and Supervisor, National Institute for the Psychotherapies Training Institute (NIP); Faculty and Supervisor, National Training Program for NIP; and Faculty, The Stephen Mitchell Relational Study Center. She is co-Editor, with Amy Schwartz-Cooney of the collection: Vitalization in Psychoanalysis, and maintains a private practice in New York City.

This response to Koshkarian and Larralde first clarifies the term “kinship object” placing it within its cultural and psychoanalytic context, then offers an alternative theoretical conceptualization based on the developmental achievement of the internalized good object as a necessary prerequisite for the experience of kinship. The response then connects the idea of kinship to the thinking of Searles and his work on the nonhuman environment. These themes emphasize the importance of the unconscious, its centrality to the psychoanalytic project, and our responsibility as psychoanalysts to bring the dark undercurrents of repressed thoughts and feelings into our awareness,

[[RunningHead]]

The Innateness of Kinship Object

Lisa Koshkarian PHD and Chris Larralde BFA

Chris Larralde

2211 Post Street, #314, San Francisco, CA 94115–3427
drlisakosh@yahoo.com

Chris Larralde   Graduate of the University of Washington School of Design and Photography and currently serves as a Creative Director for tech companies, where he crafts innovative branding systems and design solutions. Outside of his work, he dedicates time to composing and playing classical and rock guitar music, practicing internal martial arts, and meditation. Additionally, he nurtures his garden and enthusiastically explores both eastern and western philosophies as an avid independent student. Influenced by his Japanese heritage, Chris aspires to a world characterized by increased acceptance, peace, and harmony.

Neither here nor there, I (Chris) came into the world through the omnipresent duality of my Japanese mother and my mixed European father who married 20 years after the end of WWII. My mother was born in the internment camps where all Japanese Americans were detained due to racial and political fear. Meanwhile, her father was fighting in the 442nd Infantry Regiment (the most decorated unit of the war) for the very government that had imprisoned his family. My father’s Basque heritage is tied to one of the oldest ethnic groups in Europe. Nestled in the Pyrenees mountains between France and Spain, Basque history is marked by struggles for selfhood while being subjected to the civil wars and control dynamics of its neighboring nations. As a result, the Basques are a people fighting for autonomy with an identity that is French, Spanish, and distinctly Basque. These contradictory and hypocritical cultural outcomes, products of fear and perception, created experiences and a sense of displacement that shaped my worldview. As an only child, I was keenly aware of my biracial identity, feeling half but not whole. I envied the collective busyness of larger families where many “pieces” seemed more unified than my bifurcated, yet singular existence. This sense of disjointedness led me to ponder the nature of reality, which I see as a koan of push/pull and expand/collapse dynamics that eventually invert through paradoxes to reveal an underlying truth. I am reminded of how we ended the last World War in an attempt to unify by splitting a singular thing – an atom. As we navigate perilous times, I hope that the plague of “otherness” may resolve itself through a kinship process of unification and wholeness rather than another catastrophic split.

Genocide lives in my (Lisa’s) DNA. My mother is 100% German Jewish. My father is full-blooded Armenian. Between 1915 and 1923, Turks of the Ottoman Empire undertook a propaganda campaign to convince the public that Armenians presented a dire threat to the Ottomans’ security. This resulted in massacre of over a million Armenians. Turks and Armenians share similar racial and cultural profiles. Yet one group with the ability and wherewithal to organize their destructive impulses determined that the other group must be extinguished. I visited Istanbul in 1986. I brought the half of myself that had been deemed a century prior as welcome as a rabid dog. On a Munich trip a few years later, what lived in my body was the awareness that the other half of myself, and therefore my entire personhood, had been classified as dirty and inhuman in the late 30s and 40s. A mere 15 miles from the brew pubs where I enjoyed schnitzel and Pilsner is Dachau. My great aunt and uncle perished there at the hands of the Nazis. Jewish and non-Jewish Germans share potent cultural and national commonalities, and yet the arbitrariness of the emperor commanding that his new clothes be broadly revered in lockstep led to the extermination of six million Jews. How do I reconcile that my ancestors’ otherness and sameness vis a vis their fellow national citizens represented not an opportunity for affinity and kinship, but cause for

[[RunningHead]]

“A Woman Who Found She Needed a Session of Indefinite Length”: A Look at Chapter Four of Winnicott’s Playing and Reality Through the Prism of its Modalities of Temporality

Smadar Steinbock MA

Smadar Steinbock

Tel-Aviv, Israel
smadarstei@gmail.com

Tel-Aviv, Israel

Smadar Steinbock   Clinical Psychologist and a Training Psychoanalyst in the Israel Psychoanalytic Society and in the IPA. She is past President of the Israel Psychoanalytic Society (2017-2020). She is on the teaching faculty at the Psychoanalytic Institute and at the Israel Winnicott Center. Head of the Psychoanalytical-Psychotherapy Programm in Winnicot Center. She is in full-time private practice in psychoanalysis and psychotherapy in Tel-Aviv, working with children, adolescents and adults. Her papers: ‘The Point of Non-Disturbance’ (2017) and ‘Reflections on “A Woman who Found she Needed a Session of Indefinite Length”’ (2023), were published in Hebrew in ‘Sihot’- Israeli Journal of Psychotherapy. She is co-editor of the translation to Hebrew of Loewald’s writings, a book in process.

This paper offers a reflection and an open, experiential reading of one of Winnicott’s most fascinating case studies. I try to show how the question of time in the psychoanalytical context emerges not only in relation to a deviation from the traditional setting but also concerns an axis inherent to some key therapeutic issues, such as the meaning of time and the temporal as a quality of holding in regression to dependency; or as a component in the transference experience or in the therapist’s holding in mind of the patient’s potential, through holding the future. The high frequency of meetings in psychoanalysis facilitates qualitative change, allowing patients and therapists to be in touch with deep, primitive strata of the psyche. Extending time in psychoanalytic treatment, therefore, whether as an ad hoc response to the patient’s needs or as an extension of the setting, is tantamount to enabling a multi-layered experience of time across the spectrum of its manifestations. Attention to the temporal component in therapy and the experience of time in the transference is a valuable resource in our work. This paper contributes to the attempt to elaborate this dimension of psychoanalytic listening.

The discussions of Steven Cooper and Peter Goldberg, so rich and inspiring, provide a fruitful interplay with the text, and offer an opening for new thought and further elaboration. I will relate to some of these, zooming in on how the therapist meeting a patient’s profound need (as described in the Chapter I discussed in my paper) may occur in the temporal modality of keeping the future in mind.

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 34-6

Stephen Hartman PHD , Amy Cooney PHD , Jack Foehl PHD and Lauren Levine PHD

Lauren Levine

Lauren Levine  

It’s become an unofficial ritual. Every two months for the past four years, we four editors in chief turned our attention to this column. As we’ve written before, our original intention was (and remains) to use In This Issue to chronical the journal’s manner of decision-making and self-examination. As we have encountered process issues, ethical questions, controversial policy choices, or simply perplexing editorial decisions, we have used this column to elaborate how we resolved matters so that each issue came together as a step toward shaping a joint mission. When there was an incident to respond to, or a world event that captured our attention, we often linked the articles In This Issue to whatever that prompt brought to our minds.

We’ve tried to avoid being thematically repetitive, yet often as now, the troubled world we find ourselves in and the deep psychoanalytic issues and split complementarities that our current context has brought to the fore, are painfully recalcitrant. At times like this, we often wax personal and then wonder how to map our personal reactions onto a story about the journal’s ongoing being.

This installment of In This issue began as a goodbye conversation. We met as a team of four joint editors for the last time and marveled at how much we have been able to accomplish in terms of broadening the journal’s reach while also taking account of the importance of our shared legacy. When we began working together, the four of us each had a particular angle on psychoanalytic theory and practice that we brought to the table. At this moment, when Jack Foehl and Amy Schwartz Cooney are about to step down, we realize how much we have grown through difference and dialogue to the point that our angles now seem something more like vectors tracing ever-broadening arcs of relational perspective. When we were recently asked to review proposals for a symposium, it struck us that authors proposed innovations based solely on political commitments as if the history of psychoanalytic ideas were not a point from which innovation might emanate. In turn, other authors worked to deepen foundational concepts as if doing so were immune to context.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking About the Unthought Multiracial Subject: A Response to Adam Rodríguez’s “Disallowing Multiplicity: Internalized Hierarchies, Dissociation, and Unformulated Bits of Self in a Poor, Mixed-Race Kid”

Mary Kim Brewster PHD

Mary Kim Brewster

New York, United States
drmarykimbrewster@gmail.com

Mary Kim Brewster   The guest editor of the special edition of Studies in Gender and Sexuality, 25(4): “On Being Asian in America,” and an associate editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. She is a clinical psychologist, a faculty member of The Center for Asian American Psychoanalysis, and the Director of the Serious Mental Illness and the Family Project at the Ackerman Institute for the Family in New York. Brewster supervises in the Doctoral Program in Clinical Psychology at the City University of New York and has a private practice in New York.

In responding to Adam Rodríguez’s paper in this issue, I offer three autobiographical vignettes to depict aspects of my subjective experience of living as a multiracial person. Using his discussion of the multiracial subject as a point of departure, I consider how Asibong, Eng and Han, Stephens, and Mura conceptualize the racialized subject as socialized into carrying the harmful effects of racism for a White hegemonic system that is invested in erasing and forgetting its painful and violent history. I join Rodríguez in thinking about how a psychoanalytic treatment that combines an analysis of the racialized transference and countertransference, along with an exploration of the deleterious effects of the day-to-day racism experienced by the multiracial subject, opens possibilities to metabolize anger and guilt and mitigate the dissociation of aspects of the multiracial self.

Per (as in, by Means of) Versions are Us

Elizabeth Corpt MSW L.I.C.S.W.

Elizabeth Corpt

Brookline, Massachusetts, USA
ecorpt@comcast.net

Elizabeth Corpt   Practicing psychoanalyst in Arlington, Massachusetts. She is President Emerita, faculty member, and supervising analyst at the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis, an Editor Emerita of Psychoanalysis, Self and Context. Her published writings include a focus on psychoanalysis and social class, and the interface between ethical, clinical, and relational/intersubjective concerns.

In his challenge to psychoanalysis to reach beyond its normative, binary, and homogeneous tilt, Rodríguez fruitfully employs the use of a both/neither lens. This inspired this author to begin by looking back at the conventional work of Chasseguet-Smirgel on perversion and creativity, and the long shadow it has caste on psychoanalysis and the culture. The author suggests that it is the very welcome widening scope of psychoanalytic practitioners, including Rodríguez, who are creatively rethinking, reworking, and enlarging our understanding of, not only perversion, but race, class, gender, sexuality, intersectionality, trauma, and mixing. The author questions whether we have inadvertently, or unconsciously, laid out a both/neither welcome mat, thereby discouraging potential future candidates with much to offer from applying for training.

The “Unthought Mixt Subject” and Destroying Reality: Reply to Brewster and Corpt

Adam J. Rodríguez Psy.D.

Adam J. Rodríguez

833 SW 11th Avenue, Suite 414, Portland, OR 97205
USA
adam@dradamrodriguez.com

Adam J. Rodríguez   Psychoanalytic psychologist in private practice in Portland, OR. He is also an adjunct faculty at Lewis & Clark College. He is the editor and contributor of Know That You Are Worthy: Experiences From First-Generation College Graduates from Rowman & Littlefield.

In responding to the moving discussions by Mary Kim Brewster and Elizabeth Corpt, the author considers the “unthought mixt subject” and for whom it is a danger. Brewster’s notion of the “unthought mixt subject” emphasizes how mixed-race subjectivity is often overlooked within the dynamics of the interracial primal scene and in the broader context of society, including psychoanalysis. Corpt raises questions about how the psychic dilemmas of mixing, with their associated tensions and anxieties, may contribute to how analytic training makes itself untenable. The author further examines how, beyond the culture of psychoanalytic institutes, the practical aspects of training perpetuate this exclusivity. The discussion highlights how the inclination toward sameness, akin to the avoidance of difference, stifles critical thinking and provides only an illusion of comfort.

Twinship’s Dark Doppelganger: Janus-Faced Belonging Under Right-Wing Populism

Penelope Starr-Karlin Psy.D.

Penelope Starr-Karlin

2355 Westwood Blvd #825, Los Angeles, CA 90064, USA
pstarrkarlinpsyd@gmail.com

Penelope Starr-Karlin   Private practice in West LA, California and is a supervising and training analyst at ICP/LA where she teaches Stolorow and Atwood’s intersubjective-systems theory: a phenomenological contextual perspective. She is currently focusing on the psychoanalytic impact of turbulent collective contexts, especially climate change. Penelope’s work is enriched by studies in continental philosophy, Jungian psychology, and participation in study groups evolving an analytic perspective on the ecological crisis. Penelope is the author of several papers published in Psychoanalysis, Self, and Context, including The Drones of Thanatos, a response to Ukrainian analysts on the front lines; the International Journal of Psychoanalytic Self Psychology and Psychoanalytic Inquiry, and has a chapter in the forthcoming monograph Climate and Beyond in the Consulting Room.

In this discussion of the chapter by Ruganci et al. in which Kohut’s twinship transference is set in a post-democratic authoritarian national context, its unnoticed, dangerous, and dark doppelganger, polarization, and us/them politics, will be addressed. It is posited that psychoanalytic work can defuse politically manipulated binaries when a client’s Large Group Identity is reconceptualized, not as a static metaphysical object, but as the outcome of the ongoing triggering of a vulnerable preexisting archaic subjective emotional theme known as an organizing principle. Under authoritarian persuasion, the normative systems process of a subjectively unique set of organizing principles fluidly moving in and out of perception in response to changes in context, becomes dominated by one, the organizing principle of Belonging. This approach brings political manipulation into a subjective frame and the purview of psychoanalysis as illustrated by clinical cases. Exiled Turkish writer/lawyer Ece Temelkuran is introduced to describe the right-wing populist playbook.

Rediscovering a Sensitivity for the Environment in the Clinical Encounter

Susanna Federici PHD

Susanna Federici

Via Tacito 7, Rome 00193, Italy
Milan

Susanna Federici   Founding member, Faculty, and Supervising Analyst of ISIPSÉ – Institute for Self Psychology and Relational Psychoanalysis, Italy. She is Past-President of IARPP (International Association for Relational Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy) and Past Member of the International Board of IAPSP (International Association Psychoanalytic Self Psychology). She has published in many international journals, and was co-chair of the IARPP 13th Annual Conference “The Arts of Time: Relational Psychoanalysis and Forms of Vitality in Clinical Process,” held in Rome June 9-12, 2016.

Taking into consideration that the psychophysical balance of the individual is in close relationship with the collective-environmental balance, thus the topic of the environment and climate change has a strong impact on our patients and on the cultural context in which the therapies take place. Susan Bodnar’s paper is an important contribution to assuming an active role as mental health professionals facing the impact of the climate crisis in the clinical encounter. She proposes using object relations theory to develop an ecotherapy that makes use of context, integrating environmental factors into psychological well-being through therapeutic action. As Ecotherapy makes use of contexts, I suggest it could be well supported by field theory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sensitivity and Birds in a Red Sky: Finding Relationality in an Environmental Dialogue – Reply to Federici, Somers, and Stothart

Susan Bodnar PHD

Susan Bodnar

172 West 79th Street, New York, NY 10024
USA
Susanbodnarphd@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-9504-9269

Susan Bodnar   Adjunct associate professor and the PI of the DEWLab at Teachers College, Columbia University. She also serves as the clinical director of the Wellness Center in rural upstate New York and is in private practice in New York City. She is an associate editor for Psychoanalytic Dialogues and on the editorial boards of Contemporary Psychoanalysis and Ecopsychology.

This article engages with the responses of Somers, Stothart, and Federici to explore the intersection of psychology and climate change. It highlights the growing psychological fallout from environmental disasters and the efforts of professionals, particularly within the Climate Psychology Alliance, to support those affected. Despite increased awareness of the interconnection between mental health and environmental crises, the field of ecotherapy still challenges traditional psychological frameworks. Somers emphasizes ecotherapy’s potential in addressing climate trauma, drawing parallels between psychological dissociation and humanity’s disconnection from nature. Stothart further explores this theme, introducing the concept of “climate breakdown” and critiquing the separation between humans and nature, which has fragmented both our ecosystems and psyches. Federici expands the scope of this dialogue by connecting ecological degradation to the global economic systems that perpetuate it. She argues that mental health practitioners must address the psychological impact of environmental destruction, calling for an expanded theoretical framework that includes the non-human world. Together, these reflections challenge traditional psychological paradigms, advocating for a more integrated, ecologically informed approach to mental health practice. This response ultimately calls for a shift in clinical work, where ecological interdependence becomes central to understanding and treating mental health and where the boundaries of psychology expand to include the human-environment relationship.

responseSet.authorCitation: Cooney, A., Foehl, J., Hartman, S. & Levine, L.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction

Amy Schwartz Cooney PHD , Jack Foehl PHD , Stephen Hartman PHD and Lauren Levine PHD

Lauren Levine

Lauren Levine  

In his 1974 paper, Winnicott theorized Fear of Breakdown – a persistent terror of impending catastrophe that occurs as a result of traumatic, unprocessed rupture in the early mother-infant relationship. What is feared in the future has, in effect, already occurred in the past, but has never been known or truly lived. Instead, these primitive agonies manifest in tormenting anticipation of imminent disaster. As we endure these dark times and witness the rise of fascist leaders, horrific global wars, racist divisions, environmental threat, and proliferating destruction, fear of breakdown and dread of doom has become a living reality, affecting each of us in our multiple implications – not equally, but nonetheless corrosively.

For this snapshot series, we have asked a range of psychoanalytic thinkers to comment on Winnicott’s profound construct and its implications theoretically, clinically, and culturally. What follows is a remarkable and diverse collection of brief essays, in which each of our contributors offer their unique perspectives and associations to the theory of Fear of Breakdown.

Amy Schwartz Cooney, Ph.D., Jack Foehl, Ph.D., Stephen Hartman, Ph.D. and Lauren Levine, Ph.D.

Joint Editors-in-Chief, Psychoanalytic Dialogues

Circles of Sorrow: Discussion of David Eng’s and Shinhee Han’s “Racial Rage, Racial Guilt: The Uses of Anger in Asian America”

Andrew Asibong PHD

Andrew Asibong

London, UK
andrewasibongtherapy@gmail.com

Andrew Asibong   Intercultural psychodynamic psychotherapist and film theorist, whose recent publications include Post-Traumatic Attachments to the Eerily Moving Image: Something to Watch Over Me (Routledge, 2022) and “Walking on Thin Ice: Discussion of Kris Yi’s ‘Asian-American Experience: The Illusion of Inclusion and the Model Minority’”(Psychoanalytic Dialogues, 2023).

Using David Eng’s and Shinhee Han’s article in this issue as a point of departure, I respond to their discussion of Asian American subjectivity as it is represented in the Netflix series Beef. Focusing on the challenges faced by “hybrid” groups and individuals who find themselves blocked from both the expression of rage and the experience of self-representation, I reflect on the emotional and ontological vicissitudes of a range of “in-betweeners” within violently hierarchical systems, from those of mixed ethnic heritage to the invisibilized children of narcissistically abusive families.

This brief essay focuses on Adrienne Harris’s consideration of the analyst’s vocation, both as work and as a calling. Harris identifies a psychoanalytic vocation as one that is “unsettled,” leaving analysts to examine countertransference by attending not only to their own affect states, but through the exhumation of generational transfers that are informed by social histories and constructions. Harris’s generative pedagogy, rooted in her capacious comparative scholarship, stands as exemplary of the analytic vocation.

[[RunningHead]]

Destroying the “Human” for the Survival of the World: A Proposal for a Temporally Dense Psychoanalytic Subject

Katie Gentile PHD

Katie Gentile

524 W. 59th Street, New York, NY 10019
kgentile@jjay.cuny.edu

Katie Gentile   Professor of Interdisciplinary Studies at John Jay College of Criminal Justice, CUNY. She is the author of Creating bodies: Eating disorders as self-destructive survival and the 2017 Gradiva Award winning The business of being made: The temporalities of reproductive technologies, in psychoanalysis and cultures. She is editor of the journal Studies in Gender and Sexuality, on the faculties of New York University’s Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy, and the Psychoanalysis and the Critical Social Psychology program at the CUNY Graduate Center.

Despite the pandemic upending in-person clinical treatments, psychoanalysis struggles to conceptualize a frame that is truly open to a world beyond the singular, human only dyad. This article challenges psychoanalysis to blowup the frame of practice by destroying the very subject of the work - the human. Integrating key concepts from anti-blackness and indigenous theories, the author contends that the category of the human is inherently and irreparably violent; the undergirding onto-epistemological system for the formation of white settler colonial, heteropatriarchal subjectivity and its co-created violences of enslavement, dispossession, genocide, mass extinctions, and ecological destruction. Because psychoanalysis has evolved around this Enlightenment subject that renders certain people “human,” and all others disposable, its frame and focus is designed to uphold, not resist, biopolitical and ecological violence. Thus, psychoanalysis is onto-epistemologically unfit and unable to effectively address social violences and the climate crisis while emanating from this exceptionalist “human.” Integrating anti-blackness and indigenous theories, the author, using examples from clinical work online, proposes a different form of subjectivity, one that is co-emergent with “dense temporalities” of the more-than-human. Only through such a temporally dense, transcorporeal subject can psychoanalysis hope to subvert the world of human destruction.

Playing in Time: A Discussion of Smadar Steinbock’s “A Look at Chapter Four of Winnicott’s Playing and Reality Through the Prism of its Modalities of Temporality”

Steven H. Cooper PHD

Steven H. Cooper

USA
275 Central Park West, 1C, New York, NY 10024
stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven H. Cooper   Clinical Professor in Psychiatry at the Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research and Adjunct Professor at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis. He is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute and at the Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. He is Chief Editor Emeritus of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. His most recent book Playing and Becoming in Psychoanalysis was published by Routledge in 2023. In 2025, Routledge is also publishing two forthcoming books, Psychoanalysis in Play: Reconsidering Psychoanalytic Concepts from a Play Perspective as well as Beyond Playing and Dreaming: Winnicott’s 1967 Letter to Bion (S.H. Cooper and C.G. Lovett editors).

Winnicott’s paper, “Playing, Creativity, and the Search for the Self,” Chapter 4 of Playing and Reality is the second leg of Winnicott’s two-part opus on play. It is the focus of Smadar Steinbock’s essay on temporality in the work of Winnicott and Loewald. Winnicott’s is a paper fundamentally about play. I suggest that for Winnicott, play always involves time, specifically the actual or feared loss of time or object, namely what I have referred to as the play of mourning. For Winnicott, temporality is inextricably bound with his notion of place. And they are both contained in the analytic setting. This is a setting that allows for the emergence of play and creative experiences of self, in time in general and for the time being. Like so many phenomena in Winnicott’s theory building, time involves both actual or real time and time as incorporated psychically into the realm of illusion. Chapter 4 puts forward the ways that patient and analyst hold the paradox of time as actual and as incorporated into the realm of psychic illusion in order for the patient to be found.
